narrative:
Yesterday was among the coolest high temperatures ever recorded on May 14; only 1954 was significantly cooler. Deeper valleys are below freezing early this morning, but morning sunshine and a southwest flow quickly flip that to a pleasant afternoon. Mother Nature broke the "Mother's Day Rule" for frost this year, but this is the last really chilly shot of spring. Warmer weather arrives quickly this weekend and holds into next week. The only potential for rain is a stray shower late Sunday afternoon.

Winter Snow Recap

With the last significant cold weather behind us, we released a recap of snowfall for Winter 2025-26. With the exception of a couple of outliers, the RaysWeather Fearless Forecast released back in October was about as good as it gets, with most of our forecast locations within 25% of their forecast totals.

2027 Blue Ridge Photo Winners Announced

The votes are in, and the winning photos have been selected. The Grand Prize Winner is a fall photo by Jason Frye of Price Lake looking toward Grandfather Mountain. See this and all the other winners at our Photo Contest Page.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Thursday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion The weather pattern is undergoing a big flip. An upper-level low is over New Jersey this morning; it races northeast during the day. Warm high pressure builds into the western Atlantic and extends back into the Eastern U. S. for several days. After a chilly start to Friday, temperatures warm nicely during the day with lots of sunshine. Add another 8 degrees or so on Saturday. Then Sunday through Tuesday feel like summer. A shower will be very difficult to find through Tuesday, but I cannot completely rule out a stray shower or thundershower Sunday or Tuesday afternoons. A cold front approaches from the northwest Wednesday and moves through Thursday. Expect scattered showers and thundershowers Wednesday afternoon and still possible Wednesday night. Scattered showers and thundershowers remain in play through Thursday. Temperatures will be less warm Wednesday and cooler Thursday. Submitted by Grace P. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Winter 2025-26 Recap Another winter has come and gone. We’ve crossed over into spring, and some spots in the Foothills have already notched their first 90 degree day of 2026. While there will still yet be a couple chilly evenings in store for the mountains, history tells us that snowflakes are almost certainly a thing of the past until next winter. This winter (and fall, and spring), the region recieved snowflakes first on November 10th, and the final flakes fell as flurries on April 20th. With all of this in mind, let’s take a look back at what was the winter of 2025-2026 in Southern Appalachia. First of all, we want to take a transparent look at how Ray's 2025-26 Fearless Winter Forecast fared.

Hendersonville 7.7 7 -10% -0.7 6.7 114.9
Hickory 8.4 4 -110% -4.4 3.8 221.1
Jefferson 15.1 15 -1% -0.1 14.4 104.9
Lenoir 8.2 4 -105% -4.2 4.1 200.0

Mt. Mitchell 50.2 70 28% 19.8 89.1 56.3
N. Wilkesboro 6.8 5 -36% -1.8 5.6 121.4
Old Fort 5.9 7 16% 1.1 8.2 72.0
Sparta 17.0 14 -21% -3.0 18.6 91.4
Spruce Pine 16.4 13 -26% -3.4 11.0 149.1
Sugar Mountain 69.5 70 1% 0.5 68.0 102.2
Waynesville 9.5 10 5% 0.5 10.9 87.2
Wolf Laurel 72.5 55 -32% -17.5 60.0 120.8
Wytheville 16.7 18 7% 1.3 18.4 90.8
What We Got Right Mountain Snow: Take a quick gander at the above table, and you will find that many of the forecasted numbers in our mountainous regions were within 25% of reality. The outliers are the high elevations of Wolf Laurel and Mount Mitchell, as well as Banner Elk. Wolf Laurel and Banner Elk exceeded expectations, as well as their average snowfall, while Mount Mitchell fell short of its average and our forecast. But, for the most part, Ray's numbers were exceptional. Sugar Mountain was within a half inch of forecast for the entire winter - you just cannot do it any better than that for a seasonal forecast. Boone exceeded the forecast by just 2 inches, while Mt. Airy and Hendersonville were both within an inch of the forecasted total. Winter Temperatures: Back in October, Ray predicted near seasonal average temperatures. The map below shows Western North Carolina just barely on the side of below normal for the three month period of December, January, and February. Many locations within our region fell within a degree of average for that period. You may think that it is a less impressive feat to say that temperatures over a three month period will be roughly what they usually are, and while it is not as good as getting seasonal snow totals within an inch, take a look at the northeastern and western U.S. Those regions fell well below and well above normal for winter, and our forecast correctly predicted we would land roughly in the middle of that battle of warm versus cold. What We Got Wrong Foothills Frenzy: The number one thing that sticks out like a sore thumb on the table above is the Foothills. The highest percentage errors belong to Hickory, Lenoir, and Morganton, who all recieved twice as much (or more) snowfall than usual. This does come with a bit of a caveat: the Foothills have rather low annual snowfall averages (less than 4 inches for Hickory), making it rather easy to exceed those averages by a large percentage with a single snowstorm. That was the case in 2026, when a statewide system dropped a widespread 5 to 8 inches across much of the Foothills. The northern Foothills recieved less snow relative to average than areas closer to Interstate 40, simply because that one event produced more snow for Hickory and Morganton than Wilkesboro. You can see that in the forecast error, with North Wilkesboro at 36% and Morganton at 114%. Morganton also recieved an extra inch or so with a second, brief snowfall a few days after the big one to boost them further past forecast. The Snow Spotters This was the first winter of a revamped Snowman-O-Mometer Super Snow Spotters network with Ray's Weather, and it was a resounding success. Over the course of this winter, we recieved 3,171 individual reports from 173 different spotters! With the addition of the ability to submit photos, 598 were shared from our spotters. Before we continue, here's a quick look at some of our favorite spotter-submitted photos from this winter. The beauty of our home never ceases to amaze. Another thing amazes us is the snow totals from Wolf Laurel during the big snowstorm that ended January: 15 to 20 inches! Those were our heaviest reports for the entire winter, and were likely the heaviest single-storm amounts anywhere in the region. Although, it is possible the far reaches of the Smokies or the Roan Highlands found a way to notch such totals. The level of commitment and detail we recieved from our snow spotters was incredible, and we are very grateful for their contributions to Ray's Weather. We look forward to more winters ahead with them! Storm-By-Storm Looking back on the individual systems that came through our backyard this winter, five of them stand out as the heaviest and most impactful of the season. Each of these bullet points links to our snow spotter totals and recap of each system. Early November Winter Blast December 8th Snow January Icy Mess Statewide Snowstorm Heavy February Mountain Snow Each of these storms brought at least 5 inches of snow and/or ice to at least one or two of our spotters, and for that reason they stand out among the 20+ events of this winter. From the exceptional cold and snow near Veteran's Day (8 to 10+ inches at high elevations) to the beautiful, heavy & wet snow of December 8th in the High Country (a Watauga & Ashe Co. special; 5 to 8"), and the icy mess of late January that left a miniature glacier on many less-frequented roads, these are the ones to remember from 2025-26. Crunching Numbers As meteorologists and climatologists, there is all sorts of fun post-winter analysis one can get pulled into. In writing this recap, we found a few interesting data points to list: Beech Mountain reported 12.0 inches of snowfall with the statewide snowstorm on Feburary 1st. That was the first single event report of 12.0 inches or greater in over two full years, dating back to January 17th, 2024. Over the course of 36 winters on record at Beech, that was the 3rd longest stretch between 12-inch snowfalls. Lenoir reported 6.5" with the same system, which was the first snowfall in excess of 3 inches since January 17th, 2022. It was also only the 6th 6-inch snowfall since the turn of the century! This was Asheville's 8th consecutive winter with below average snowfall, which is 11.5 inches. In 2017-18, 13.0 inches was the last total to exceed the average. Over 26 winters in the 21st century, this winter ranked as the 17th snowiest. While there is still some time left, it appears Boone may go the length of April without a snowflake flying. Despite an April average of 2.1 inches of snow, 64 of 98 Aprils on record (that's 65%) had less than an inch of snow, and 29 of 98 (just under 30%) had no snow whatsoever. So, not as abnormal as the 2.1 inch average may have you believe. Despite being roughly 7 miles apart, 2500 feet in elevation difference between Wolf Laurel and Burnsville, resulted in an incredible 50-inch difference in seasonal snowfall at the two locations. 70" at Wolf Laurel, and 20" along the Cane River. Our snow spotter near Spruce Pine recieved snowflakes 17 different times this winter, but only had 11.4 inches of snow to show for it, 6" of which came in one event! If you love flurries but don't want so much snow on the ground, move to Spruce Pine. So, there you have it. The winter of 2025-2026 smothered, covered, and chunked. Hopefully everyone found this deep dive informative as well as encouraging that your friendly neighborhood meteorologists really do know what they're doing. Until next winter! May 15, 2026 WWR? Price Lake! May 13, 2026 May Chill Continues It was another chilly start to the day, with areas of valley frost in the mountains. We have a similar bout of chill Friday morning, before we quickly toggle into a summertime weather pattern over the upcoming weekend. May 12, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
